project_overview.project_development_objective: To expand access to broadband internet, enhance digital skills and improve government capacity to deliver public services digitally
project_overview.beneficiaries: All people in Sierra Leone including individuals, businesses, and government; specifically targeting youth aged 15-35, women, persons with disabilities (PWDs), rural communities, and underserved populations

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on rural and underserved areas
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Sierra Leone is post-conflict with fragility context; project addresses digital divides in fragile areas through rural connectivity and inclusive access components

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Expanding Digital Access, and Increasing Resilience of the Digital Environment
key_components.description: Improve broadband market competition, expand access to broadband internet, and address digital divides through policy/regulatory support, last-mile connectivity for public institutions, rural connectivity via reverse auctions, assistive technology for PWDs, cybersecurity enhancement, climate resilience, and e-waste management

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Digital Skills Development and Innovation
key_components.description: Develop digital literacy and skills training for youth aged 15-35 (targeting women and PWDs), conduct market studies on digital skills supply/demand and gig economy, implement digital jobs pilots, support innovation events (hackathons, bootcamps), and develop drone ecosystem for innovation and climate applications

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Laying Key Foundations for Digital Government Services and Systems
key_components.description: Establish enabling policy/legal/regulatory environment for digital government, develop digital service standards, implement enterprise architecture and interoperability frameworks, strengthen government networks and data infrastructure, establish government notification platform, pilot digital access points, and develop G2C/G2B/G2G digital services (e-Cabinet, e-Parliament, e-Justice blueprint)

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Project Management and Implementation Support
key_components.description: Finance project coordination unit staffing, procurement, financial management, monitoring and evaluation, environmental and social safeguards, citizen engagement, grievance redress mechanism, and capacity building for government officials

Provide flexibility for rapid response to eligible crises (climate/natural disasters, public health emergencies) through reallocation of uncommitted balances from other components; covers emergency payments, continuity of government functions, and crisis response expenditures
key_components.financing: US$0.0 million (zero funding at appraisal, can be activated)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Information and Communications (MIC) with dedicated Project Coordination Unit (PCU) staffed with Project Coordinator, Deputy Coordinator, M&E Specialist, Procurement Specialist, Financial Management Specialist, and Environmental and Social Safeguards Specialist
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (co-chaired by Ministers of Finance and MIC) for strategic guidance; Technical Committee (chaired by MIC Minister or delegated senior official) for problem-solving; Technical Lead Agencies (DSTI, NATCOM, UADF, MIC e-government teams) providing technical expertise; coordination with development partners (UN Foundation DIAL, UNICEF, Gates Foundation)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations (November 2020); Sierra Leone National Public Procurement Act 2016 for national market tenders; use of Standard Procurement Documents; mandatory use of STEP (Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement); international competitive bidding preferred for ICT services; reverse auction mechanism for rural broadband subsidies; demand aggregation for last-mile connectivity; prior and post review of contracts based on High procurement risk rating
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PCU M&E Specialist responsible for results tracking by component with support from Technical Lead Agencies; biannual progress reports to World Bank within 45 days of June and December; annual audited financial statements within 6 months of fiscal year end; internal audit quarterly reports; external audit annually; monitoring of PDO indicators (broadband access, digital skills, government capacity) and intermediate results indicators by component; citizen feedback mechanism and grievance redress system with 90% service standard for response

identified_risks.risk_description: General election scheduled for early 2023 during project's first year could cause unforeseen incidents, slowdown in procurement due to delays in internal clearance, difficulty organizing timely PSC/TC meetings, and potential policy reversal or political interference
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close World Bank implementation support; advance preparation of annual work plans and time-bounded action plans; robust procurement approach with transparent international competitive bidding processes; clear articulation of consequences for non-conformance; strong Project Steering Committee with key MDAs well represented

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 pandemic, Ukraine war impacts (inflation surge to 17.6% in February 2022), supply chain disruptions, rising food prices, large current account deficit (13% of GDP), and fiscal consolidation challenges will affect project operational costs and allocated budgets
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Government commitment to fiscal discipline and macroeconomic reforms; implementation of World Bank Productivity and Transparency Support Development Policy Financing; IMF Extended Credit Facility program; real GDP growth projected at 3.4% in 2022 with gradual recovery; fiscal deficit expected to decline to 2.4% of GDP by 2023

identified_risks.risk_description: Cross-cutting digital transformation covers multiple new interventions targeting various demographic groups across wide range of beneficiary institutions; challenges in ensuring suitability and customization for subgroups; limited MIC experience implementing large-scale World Bank projects; pressure to implement within 5-year horizon
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Phased project design with sequenced implementation of components from outset; sequencing of key activities discussed and incorporated during preparation; World Bank intensive implementation support including hands-on training and workshops

identified_risks.risk_description: Government needs to acquire new expertise and move toward standardized integrated digital infrastructure from fragmented siloed IT investment; implementation requires coordination across multiple MDAs; limited institutional capacity for digital transformation leadership
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive capacity-building program embedded in project to upskill government officials; Component 1 supports NATCOM institutional capacity for transition to NCA with wider regulatory mandate; Component 3 supports MIC e-government unit capacity development for planning and coordination; Component 4 establishes robust PCU with strong implementation capacity; World Bank intensive implementation support

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited fiduciary capacity in MIC; potential long duration for FM/procurement staff recruitment; FM and procurement risks rated Substantial; limited knowledge of World Bank Procurement Regulations and STEP; inefficiencies and delays in procurement; insufficient competition; weak complaint redress system
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Competitive recruitment of qualified FM and procurement staff for PCU; regular capacity training on World Bank procedures; establishment of good record management system; regular procurement clinics and training; aggregation of smaller packages where feasible; sensitization of private sector to bid; biannual reporting on complaints; World Bank hands-on support in procurement and financial management; regular FM reviews; independent external audit; internal audit with quarterly reports; audit committee oversight

identified_risks.risk_description: Wide range of stakeholders and inter-ministerial coordination required; cross-cutting nature adds complexity; deep-rooted trust issues between Government and telecom operators; need for whole-of-government digital transformation approach
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Effective implementation of Stakeholder Engagement Plan; strong coordination and stakeholder management throughout implementation; close collaboration with Technical Lead Agencies and beneficiary agencies; joint industry approach for IXP to build trust with private sector; close collaboration with UN Foundation DIAL for digital government support; establishment of strong PSC and functional TC; regular stakeholder consultations

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of exclusion of vulnerable populations (youth, women, PWDs, hard-to-reach communities) due to digital literacy gaps, high telecom tariffs, lack of affordable internet, lack of electricity in rural areas, lack of assistive devices; potential for land acquisition/displacement from last-mile connectivity installations; labor-related health and safety risks; SEA/SH risks in construction and training settings
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Targeted digital literacy training for youth, women, girls, PWDs; purchase of assistive technology equipment for OPDs; last-mile connectivity at local level with community-based DAPs; financing schemes for low-income households; innovative business models for rural broadband; site-specific E&S assessments before civil works; contractor ESMPs with SEA/SH risk assessment; SEA/SH Action Plan; Labor Management Procedures; codes of conduct; worker and community training; GBV-sensitive GRM procedures; referral pathways for service providers

identified_risks.risk_description: Construction waste from last-mile connectivity and digital infrastructure installation; e-waste generation from increased digital devices and systems; energy resources required for IT equipment and telecom facilities; potential nuisance from air/noise emissions; waste disposal and management; traffic management; occupational health and safety risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Generic E-Waste Management Plan to be prepared before implementation of activities generating e-waste; e-waste policy framework development based on circular economy approach; 3R strategy (reduce, recycle, reuse); site-specific ESMPs for contractors with cost-effective mitigation measures; regular supervision; Good International Industry Practices for energy sourcing and use; community health and safety risk management; traffic management plans; occupational health and safety procedures

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS4)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level GRM established and centralized within PCU to collect and respond to complaints, queries, suggestions, and compliments from beneficiaries, system users, stakeholders, and general population; service standard of 90% feedback addressed and responded within stipulated timeframes; links between GRM and M&E systems; biannual citizen engagement feedback reports published; World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available for project-affected communities; World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints regarding World Bank noncompliance
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Comprehensive gender analysis identifying gaps in digital literacy, access to training, and economic participation; targeted actions include: flexible training hours and safe locations for women; women-only training cohorts; on-site childcare and transportation/meal stipends; female trainers and mentors; free training programs; digital jobs pilots with continued support; awareness campaigns on benefits of digital skills; cybersecurity and online safety training including protection against online violence against women; 40% female target for digital skills training and 40% for digital jobs pilots; gender-sensitive GRM procedures; women's participation in decision-making bodies; addressing barriers to recruitment, retention, and promotion of women in government

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project addresses fragility through digital inclusion and economic opportunity creation; digital skills training and employment opportunities target youth (75% of population under 35 years) to address high youth unemployment as source of fragility; broadband expansion and digital services improve government service delivery and citizen engagement, strengthening state capacity and legitimacy; digital government systems enhance transparency and reduce corruption; early warning systems for climate disasters improve resilience; project design incorporates lessons from WARCIP Sierra Leone to avoid political interference and conflicts of interest through robust procurement and transparent processes
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sierra Leone is post-conflict with multiple development challenges including poverty (43% below poverty line), weak institutions, North-South ethnic/regional divide, high youth unemployment, undiversified economy dependent on agriculture and natural resources, vulnerability to exogenous shocks (commodity prices, weather, health, global inflation); approximately 75% of population under 35 years with 40% under 15 years; high youth unemployment associated with low skills and unmet expectations identified as important source of fragility; government working to maintain macroeconomic stability post-Ebola and COVID-19; debt distress risk; inflation challenges (17.6% in February 2022)
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project includes cybersecurity enhancement through Critical Information Infrastructure Protection (CIIP) plans for priority sectors (financial services, government, telecommunications, energy, aviation); operationalization of National Cybersecurity Coordination Center (NCCC) and National Computer Incident Response Team (N-CIRT); cybersecurity skills development and online safety awareness; Cybersecurity Maturity Model assessment; climate early warning systems for disaster risk management; private and public security personnel may be used to guard project sites with measures to manage security risks through contractor ESMPs; no armed or military force will be used
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Minor risk expected - Last-mile connectivity and IT equipment installation at selected public institutions (government offices, local councils, hospitals, schools, community-based DAPs) may involve minor land acquisition or restrictions on land use; Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared to address potential displacement; site-specific E&S assessments required before civil works to identify and mitigate land-related impacts; contractual provisions with private sector ensure adequate E&S risk assessment and mitigation; risk of displacement assessed as minimal due to small to medium-scale infrastructure works and site-specific nature of impacts
